g.data-narration: Shared memory is a region of physical memory mapped into multiple process address spaces, enabling direct data exchange without kernel involvement.
g.text: A memory region mapped into address spaces of multiple processes
Allows direct read/write without kernel for data transfer
Fastest form of IPC - no copying between address spaces

g.data-narration: Shared memory offers superior performance because processes access data directly, making it ideal for large data transfers between related processes.
System V IPC provides standardized shared memory operations: shmget creates segments, shmat attaches them, shmdt detaches, and shmctl controls them.
g.text: Four key system calls manage shared memory lifecycle

g.data-narration: shmget() creates or accesses a shared memory segment. It requires a unique key, size, and permission flags to establish proper access control.
g.text: shmget() - Create or Access Shared Memory
int shmget(key_t key, size_t size, int shmflg);
• key: Unique identifier (ftok() generates from path + project_id)

g.data-narration: shmat() attaches the shared memory segment to the process address space. Setting address to NULL lets the OS choose optimal placement location.
g.text: shmat() - Attach Shared Memory to Address Space
void *shmat(int shmid, const void *shmaddr, int shmflg);

g.data-narration: shmdt() detaches memory from the address space, while shmctl() performs control operations like reading status or removing the segment permanently.
g.text: Detaches from address space | Returns 0 on success, -1 on failure
shmctl(int shmid, int cmd, struct shmid_ds *buf)

g.data-narration: POSIX shared memory provides a simpler, file-based interface using shm_open() to create objects and mmap() to map them into memory.
POSIX functions include shm_open() for object creation, mmap() for memory mapping with protection flags, and shm_unlink() for removal.
g.text: int shm_open(const char *name, int oflag, mode_t mode)
void *mmap(void *addr, size_t len, int prot, int flags, int fd, off_t offset)
Removes shared memory object | Must be called after all processes unmap

g.data-narration: Since multiple processes access shared memory concurrently, synchronization with semaphores or mutexes is essential to prevent race conditions.
g.text: Critical: Multiple processes accessing shared data simultaneously causes data inconsistency
Process A and B read same value

g.data-narration: The producer-consumer problem demonstrates shared memory synchronization: producer writes to a buffer, consumer reads from it, with semaphores managing empty/full states.
g.text: Classic synchronization problem demonstrating shared memory usage

g.data-narration: This code demonstrates System V shared memory: server creates and writes data, client attaches using the same key and reads the data.
g.text: ✓ ftok() must use SAME path and project ID
✓ Always shmdt() before exit to prevent memory leaks

g.data-narration: Remember the shared memory lifecycle: create with shmget, attach with shmat, use the memory, detach with shmdt, and remove with shmctl IPC_RMID.
Avoid common mistakes: always detach memory, use synchronization, generate consistent keys with ftok(), and check return values for errors.
g.text: Same ftok() path for all processes
